title: Entry-Level Banker
pros: Valuable experience: Working as a Relationship Banker allowed me to develop strong communication, sales, and customer service skills. It was rewarding to help clients with their financial goals and offer personalized advice. Team environment: My colleagues were supportive and knowledgeable, creating a collaborative atmosphere in the branch. There was always someone willing to offer guidance or share their expertise.
cons: Low pay: The compensation for this role was on the lower end compared to similar positions in the industry. While there were occasional bonuses or incentives tied to sales performance, it didnât fully make up for the base salary. Pressure to sell: A significant portion of the job involved pushing RBCâs productsâsuch as credit cards, loans, and investment productsâto clients. While I understand the need for a sales focus, the emphasis on hitting sales targets often felt excessive, taking away from the relationship-building aspect of the role. It became more about meeting quotas than genuinely helping clients. Limited career growth: While RBC is a large company with many opportunities, I found that the pathway for growth in this position felt somewhat limited. Advancing required significant sales success and sometimes seemed more about numbers than skill or client satisfaction.

title: Great place but bad management
pros: Great Place to Work: RBC Bank offers a positive work environment with supportive colleagues and opportunities for professional growth. Competitive Compensation: The bank provides competitive salaries and benefits, ensuring employees feel valued and well-compensated for their efforts. Professional Development: RBC Bank invests in employee development with various training programs and opportunities for career advancement. Work-Life Balance: The bank encourages a healthy work-life balance, offering flexible working hours and remote work options. Diverse and Inclusive Culture: RBC Bank promotes a diverse and inclusive workplace, fostering an environment where all employees feel welcomed and respected.
cons: Poor Management: The management at RBC Bank has been a significant drawback. They lack experience and effective strategies, especially in managing the newly formed in-house software development team. Transition Challenges: The bank has historically outsourced software development and used a waterfall methodology for project planning. This transition to an in-house team has been poorly managed, leading to inefficiencies. Lack of Trust and Autonomy: Even after hiring an in-house team, management has not fully trusted or empowered the team to take ownership of projects. This lack of autonomy hinders innovation and productivity. Resistance to Change: There is a noticeable resistance to moving away from the traditional waterfall methodology, making it difficult for the in-house team to implement more agile and modern development practices.

title: Great Company, Low Mobility
pros: RBCs benefits are very good. Their health insurance is the best I've heard of. During the pandemic they gave 2 cost of living adjustments in addition to yearly raises to help offset rising costs. The corporate culture is very friendly and un-eliteist. C level execs who have no idea who I am have casually talked to me on on elevators or while walking out the door, asking how work is going, where to go get food, or anything. And many of them are only visiting from Toronto, so it's not like they're trying to build a ground up community. I think they actually are interested in their employees.
cons: They seem to only train for the position you are in. If any sort of movement would require licensing, degrees, or so on, you only can get it once you're in that position. People in many levels seem set at their position and rarely move up, making it hard for people to get out of entry level jobs without leaving the company and coming back years later. (This is a VERY normal occurrence at RBC.) HR and internal recruiting seem very slow or overworked. I would think they would go find experienced workers internally and try to bring them into new roles, but I have never heard of anyone having this experience at RBC. (If I apply sure, but they never seen out existing talent, even when they say things like "I'll keep your resume on file.")

They are just now trying to bring things up to speed. Wages. The firm can do better. I know people who have gone 3-4 years without a pay raise. title: Salary Transparency Gone Wrong
pros: Not a lot to share
cons: As you may know, a pay transparency law went into effect in NYC in 2022. Recently, an entry-level position opened up in my immediate team, and, upon looking at the salary range, the bottom of the range was higher than I make! I discussed this with HR, and they told me that my market rate is lower than a new grad because of my "graduation year". HR refused to adjust my salary at all. I have been at RBC for 2 years with over 3 years of post-graduate experience.

title: No matter how much you work, it will never be enough
body: No matter how much you work, it will never be enough
pros: Benefits are the best PTO and work from home arrangement is nice
cons: No matter how much you work, they will always ask more from you. There is no on the job training, you will be required to figure it out as you go. The managers are so busy they have no time left to actually pay attention to their employees. You are given no goals, no direction and no feedback. You cannot say no to questions that are asked of you, you are expected to do jobs well outside of your scope. If you do need help the managers will question your commitment. Managers work 60+ hours 7 days a week and most people on the team feel pressured to do the same. The technology they roll out is terrible and you are expected to stand behind the technology that does not work at all. You spend most of your day fielding complaints that you cannot take action on. They will roll out technology they know doesn't work and just hope for the best.

title: Glad to be out.
pros: Flexible work schedule, that's all.
cons: As I writer this review, the Raleigh, NC location only has 1.3 stars. That in itself should tell you something. I was only at RBC for a short time, however, it is NOT a place I would want to go back to. Pay was decent, but the managers in my department were awful. No room for advancement, and they keep people around who constantly steal time from the company (one person was 16-20 minutes late daily, while the other was 11 minutes late daily). Under qualified people are chosen for positions based on who they know, not what they know (knowledge, experience, etc.). None of the upper level leaders seemed happy outside the stupid monthly âTown Hallsâ. Fake morale to say the least. People disappear all of sudden (resigning, medical leave, etc.). This is a classic example of, âIt looks good on the outside, but once you get in, bewareâ. My advice stay away from this place unless you are unemployed and are desperate for a job. Last, I am glad I got out.

title: AVOID IT LIKE THE PLAGUE
pros: Free coffee, casual Fridays, early dismissal on holiday weekends. And free lunch if your clients didn't eat during your meeting you get their leftovers.
cons: Extremely shady business decisions - 2 group heads were replaced by a new team from another bank without their knowledge. They were notified by clients that they were being replaced. Management did not even have the decency to notify them. Bankers are let go very often before you can bond with them. High turnover in analysts due to very long hours 9am-2am-ish. Your contract will not be renewed if you didn't brown nose. Overnighters happen often. And do not complain about anything to management because that's a problem for them and gives them extra work. MD's are either poor or amazing, very inconsistent. There are only a handful of people that are willing to be genuine mentors. Please DO NOT WORK HERE.

title: Investment Banking Analyst
pros: RBC is on par with other investment banks in terms of deal/modeling experience, upward mobility, pay, work/life balance. Having worked at other banks though, the people here really do make the difference. Maybe it's the Canadian roots, but everyone is friendly and most care about your time. Overall the culture is lively, open and keeps everyone engaged. Senior bankers care about the junior bankers and consistently hold sessions to hear our concerns and find ways to address them.
cons: We are consistently the last bank on the street to adopt new policies like increasing analyst base pay, Saturday / weekend policies, analyst to associate promotion cycles, etc. Also more technical issues than any other bank.

title: No loyalty and you will only hear crickets
body: No loyalty and you will only hear crickets
pros: I interned at RBC Wealth Management in Minneapolis, MN as a business analyst. I was in the Technical and Operations program (T&O). I worked in the Project Delivery Office (PDO). Great team and nice people for the most part. Learned great life lessons and made some great connections.
cons: No loyalty at all. All interns gave a 5 minute end of internship presentation. I was told this presentation would help us land a direct hire job if it went well. I felt like my presentation was definitely a lot better than most of the other interns. Months went by and I have not heard anything from RBC. One guy that rambled on and went over the time limit of the presentation got hired while I was left jobless. I felt like I did a much better job than him. Sure the presentation was only a part of the hiring factor but I was left hanging from the company. Didn't hear any feedback about my internship performance and the presentation at all. Attempts to contact the company were left with crickets. I also applied to a few business Analyst roles but never heard anything back. I have experience within the company but that doesn't matter to them.

title: Global Credit / Corporate Banking
pros: Relaxed office environment, exposure to other capital market desks and ability to build relationships across the bank. Constant deal flow which exposes you to a large part of midstream space.
cons: Lack of promotion opportunities; top heavy office with no turnover at senior levels (who are almost exclusively Canadian); appalling lack of expertise and leadership at senior levels results in low to no business growth; complex or high involvement transactions are limited; palpable baseless favoritism; inundated with office politics that creates have and have-nots; annoying Canadian counterparts that think they are know-it-alls and consistently try to undermine work; compensation bears little correlation with work product, revenue or return metrics; corporate culture is annoyingly PC and run by airheads; limited growth opportunities, training or mentoring.
role: Former employee - Vice President Corporate Banking

title: My experience at RBC Insurance
pros: The company really believes in empowering its people to do what needs doing. See something that's not working correctly? It will get fixed without blaming the person who found it. They also believe in work/life balance. Senior Management is in touch with the rank and file; they know what's going on day to day.
cons: I worked on the P&C side, which RBC hasn't been involved in for too long (15-20 years), and as a subsidiary of a bank, they don't really understand how catastrophes work, that sometimes you lose money due to occurrences outside your control (like the weather), and you can't live or die on one year's results.

title: Change is constant
pros: Excellent career development experience Worked on top experience-building projects Worked in a tight-knit Caribbean team and with very knowledgeable and friendly Toronto staff
cons: Second boss did not fully understand the environment, preferring instead to impose Canadian approaches, whether they fit or not Change is constant in this organisation. While at one time there was a dedicated department to handle change, the company has never fully settled down since RBC bought RBTT in 2008, leading to discomfort for staff and many negative reviews from clients.

cons: New CFO in NY about a decade ago has changed professional work environment to mafia house. Controller/Assistant controller and many good people all left and vacancies were filled with his favorites flatterer based on friendship rather than professional work skills. Current management is very political, conniving and lacks ethical value. Innocent becomes convicted and wrongdoers get praised as long as they whisper into management's ear. Always remember, good professional people surround a great leader, but political, flatterers surround a dictator.

cons: RBC is a mid-sized bank and looks to be stuck in this position for the foreseeable future.

title: Great place
pros: Great place to work and provides a great work life balance. Great people to work with and a great place if you're looking to climb the ladder
cons: It's no longer RBC bank, PNC purchased them in 2012. Corporate office is no longer Atlanta, but the office is very nice.

title: Nowhere to go
pros: Work/life balance, not much else
cons: There are no growth opportunities. Many associates have been in the same role for 10+ years and are stagnant. The same doesn't work for other associates with ambition or higher career goals, but management expects that you should be happy to have a job. Technology is completely outdated.

title: Bad Stay Away. they will fire you for younger employees
body: Bad Stay Away. they will fire you for younger employees
pros: great facilities and corporate environment
cons: will eventually move positions from NY to Toronto. They hire on the cheap there and get rid of experienced employees in NY

title: Senior Consultant - Operational Readiness
pros: Everyone works collaboratively across the Enterprise and Capital Markets
cons: Enterprise adaptation will present challenges

title: Analyst
pros: Experienced leadership. Compensation is decent. Interesting niche clientele.
cons: A small bank with the overhead of a large bank due to the relationship with the parent. Expect long hours and a what are you doing for me tomorrow attitude. Advancement is difficult as external candidates seem to be preferred to internal candidates. Little time for development opertunities due to the heavy regular work load. At no other company have I seen office politics as cut throat as RBC.

title: Good place to work
pros: * work-life balance, not too stressful * satisfactory compensation
cons: * technology is not the best * slightly outdated policies

title: Cross Border Morgage Advisor
pros: Good benefits. Unfortunately that's where it ends if you are in a sales role.
cons: I have never seen an organization that hates the sales staff as is the case at RBC Bank. They constantly reduce the staff yearly while continue to increase the goals. While sales people constantly get reduced they will turn around and hire more audit, risk and anything else that is a cost to the company while the department (sales) that actually brings in revenue is constantly under the gun and no matter how well you do the CEO still treats you like trash.

title: great place
pros: great place to work eh?
cons: are none i know of

title: Internship
pros: I have a great group of coworkers
cons: It's a pretty small office

title: Wealth Management
pros: Open to exploring all parts of the bank, not just the one I interned with. Very open and encouraging culture. Up and coming.
cons: Poor brand recognition. Hard to bring in new clients.

title: A Great Place to start your carrier or Retire with minimum skills set and efforts.
body: A Great Place to start your carrier or Retire with minimum skills set and efforts.
pros: Great benefits and excellent work environment. (Laid Back Style). I have been in finance Industry over 35 years. Never seems such a great place. (To best describe I will say LA work style in NYC/NJ) Lots of time/opportunities for full time employees to learn and grow in the field if they are correctly connect with needed political string. Great culture and very nice people around. Work and life balance. Perfect place for interns and young college graduates to learn and be ready to enter into financial world without stress and knowing too much about IT. Perfect place for the people who want to be in finance industry but don't what to deal with high pressure rewarding positions. A perfect place for job security you can retire from one place with minimum skill set. For full time employees almost impossible to get fired unless you bring down the whole bank.

title: Associate Director
pros: good company, good salary and benefits
cons: promotion process not very good

title: No Direction
pros: It's an easy-going atmosphere and the office is quiet. The laid back culture can be both good and bad, as there are extended periods where you sit around waiting to be assigned something to do.
cons: They have absolutely no concept of how to scope out a technical project. They let business analysts drive "how" instead of "what", and since they are inevitably incapable of doing that, there were hundreds of people sitting around for six months - putting in creatively-written weekly status reports explaining what had been "accomplished", when in fact, everybody was blocked on 100% of their assigned tasks for six months because of a complete and utter lack of leadership. I finally gave up (as I couldn't take going to work and having absolutely nothing to do for months on end) and left.

title: OK
pros: Smart people. Franchise is growing. Clearly committed to expanding in the US. Has some real mandates. Leveraged finance platform growing.
cons: Name still not as good as big bulge banks for analysts looking to follow 2+2 path. Some peculiarities working for a bank with head office in Canada versus US

title: Rbccm is a middle market bank that has built a business model of being on the right (non-leading).
body: Rbccm is a middle market bank that has built a business model of being on the right (non-leading).
pros: Work-life balance. Culture is relatively non-confrontational. Soft landing for senior bankers leaving bulge bracket banks or for junior bankers looking to stay in IB without having to work long hours.
cons: Lacks strong M&A platform. Rarely leads deals. Very political - you're either liked or disliked by senior members of the leveraged finance group and your rise in the firm is affected accordingly. Deals are middle of the fairway - meaning you won't be working on anything complex or above the purview of RBC's credit committee. Not a place for talented individuals looking to spread their wings. Technology is an issue. Not a place to grow a career.
role: Former employee - Vice President, Global Investment Banking

title: Mediocre management team
pros: Work life balance is decent and the location is great.
cons: Management is mediocre at best.

title: senior program leader
pros: Solid, firm with a great brand
cons: Too concerned about cutting costs versus delivering quality

title: Project Manager
pros: International company with some Canadian DNA
cons: Highly political organization. Difficult to move up on merit alone.

title: IT Analyst
pros: Above average work life balance and some attempts made for training
cons: Horrible middle and upper management. They had no strategy.

title: There's a lot of mediocrity being celebrated, and a lot of wonderful stuff being ignored or discouraged
body: There's a lot of mediocrity being celebrated, and a lot of wonderful stuff being ignored or discouraged
pros: A few really great people Hours aren't terrible

title: Senior Registered Client Associate
pros: Great company to grow your business, many resources offered.
cons: No Opportunities for advancement outside of headquarters.
role: Current employee - Senior Registered Client Associate

title: Corporate Banking Summer Analyst
pros: The bank is rapidly growing and has a laid back culture.
cons: The group is not very well respected within the bank.

title: Executive Assistant
pros: Room for growth. Many employees have been there for 10 plus years who can help mentor you. Many opportunities
cons: Corporate office setting. A little hint of old boys club.

title: Decent
pros: - Lots of resources at the disposal of an Analyst - Decent hours
cons: - Quality of deals are too simplistic for those seeking an intellectually stimulating experience - Balance sheet investment bank so proportion of M&A transactions to vanilla equity/investment grade offerings are 5:95

title: Good platform
pros: Good platform to work on, gaining market share, decent comp
cons: Little transparency in terms of promotions. Sometimes, it feels like there is too much nepotism and not enough meritocracy

title: associate
pros: Great benefits and perks and lovely commute
cons: relocated to Canada which caused rbc to lay off the operations team

title: Culture for the win
pros: Plain and simple, they don't hire jerks. If you want to work on Wall Street, love markets, and get paid like you work on Wall Street, but work in a non-hostile environment, this is your place.
cons: Brand name, people won't know where you work. People may even confuse you with RBS. Not many opportunities on the trading floor, only a few products to learn about.

title: Great Canadian Bank
pros: Great salary and offices make with the tip best place to work for its very nice an lots of growth potential
cons: No organisation in the management they are pulling in different directions and its sometimes very unhealty

title: Intern in IT
pros: Everyone I spoke to was nice and willing to help me succeed
cons: There wasn't a lot of exciting technical work being done.

title: Great company !!!
pros: Benefits Flexible Family friendly location downtown
cons: None in my experience there.

title: Vice President
pros: Great company, good pay, growing
cons: a little slow and conversative

title: Client service associate
pros: Friendly work environment but can be very cliquey.
cons: Management should recognize the support staff in more ways than just providing breakfast or lunch a few times a year. Compensation is below par, as are the bonuses. Management tends to play favorites with the support staff which leaves the rest of us feeling very undervalued. This is a tough balancing act type of position. The brokers are never wrong and management always sides with the brokers. This company is not one I would recommend, as I have worked for others, and the hierarchy was different. Currently looking for a CSA position elsewhere.
role: Current employee - Senior Registered Client Service Associate

title: CSA
pros: The office space is nice, the staff is friendly. The systems are semi-user friendly.
cons: They undervalue support staff. Managers pick and choose favorites, and spend money on things rather than giving deserving employees compensation. Lots of red tape and gossiping.
role: Former employee - Registered Client Service Associate

title: Ehhh
pros: Growing Generally pleasant people Well paid ok hours for the industry
cons: Not the most sophisticated bank low brand recognition Some awful attitudes

title: Solid, Growing Firm.... Not there yet
body: Solid, Growing Firm.... Not there yet
pros: -Growing firm -Not looking over shoulder -Decent hours for Wall Street -Good energy
cons: -Brand recognition not there yet -Hard to get out

title: Okay
pros: Greet staff Great team work Great customer service experience
cons: Low Pay Blame customer service representatives for all mistakes Cheap

title: Account Manager
pros: Great people to work with. Great information to provide to client with decent tools.
cons: Work life balance not there. Expected to hit targets even if 60+hours were needed. For the pay the time put in was not worth it.

title: Canadian I-Bank
pros: Many have worked at RBC for over 25 years. Small and trying to catchup to other I-Banks so pockets of entrepreneurialism still exist. Can make an imprint in areas and product groups that still need development.
cons: I agree with the prior posts about senior management and promotions for multiple roles globally going to Canadians as well as the notable gender bias presented under the veneer of 'RBC nice'.

title: good company
pros: best job ever - good company
cons: moved to toronto canada recently

title: Great Company
pros: Great people. Respectful workplace and low turnover rate. Puts focus on clients first. Always strive to open communication and listen to employees feedback.
cons: Not much room for career growth or slow progression. Stagnant salary improvement.

title: unix/storage administrator
pros: none - avoid this firm at all costs
cons: poor pay, useless management who talk endlessly about hoe much they value their employees all the while figuring out how to screw them some more.

title: Front Office Manager- Trade Support, Risk and Compliance
body: Front Office Manager- Trade Support, Risk and Compliance
pros: Good management, Nice colleagues, Flexible work life balance, career growth opportunities
cons: Biased Senior Management, Sexual violence at its peak, manipulation

title: Senior relationship advisor
pros: Great people and a fast paced environment. You'll never get bored here.
cons: Don't have much negative to say. Management definitely plays by the rules so expect to "dot the i's and cross the r's".

title: Vice President
pros: general relaxed environment, working hours not bad
cons: system infrastructures aren't centralized and well developed

title: Good place to work, but I have a few caveats....
body: Good place to work, but I have a few caveats....
pros: Plenty of resources to do your job. Benefits were good when I was there and there is plenty of quality individuals within the organization.
cons: The compensations system for my area was a bit of a black box. I had a very hard time figuring out what I felt the correct compensation was based on my production.

title: This is a company that compensates employees well with a good work/life balance.
body: This is a company that compensates employees well with a good work/life balance.
pros: Work/life balance, competitive compensation, company values, high integrity within the financial services industry, opportunity for personal growth, accessible corporate structure
cons: While pay is competitive, RBC is very slow to promote employees. There are not title promotions. Additionally, RBC is very slow to adapt to the changing market environment.

title: Solid Firm, Working Toward Bulge Bracket but Not Quite There
body: Solid Firm, Working Toward Bulge Bracket but Not Quite There
pros: Generally friendly culture, decent deal flow
cons: Not much M&A in investment banking, mostly capital markets, not enough lead left underwriting. A lot of co-manager roles and non-lead left bookrunning. Very long hours given that it's not quite at the very top tier of investment banks.

title: RBC is a very professional, well run company.
body: RBC is a very professional, well run company.
pros: Laid back atmosphere, great location in downtown Manhattan.
cons: Sometimes deadlines can be stressful.

title: Exciting, promising, and very inclusive.
pros: People are down-to-earth, smart, friendly, and exceptionally passionate about their work, their clients, and about RBC. The company does a lot to invest in the employees and makes you feel wanted, rewarded, and informed to the best of their ability.
cons: I believe this is more of an industry con, but too many books are done, some of which are not even required in the first place. Specifically to RBC, it is probably the best time to take lead and move officially into bulge bracket.
role: Current employee - Associate, Global Investment Banking

title: Chaotic Hell
pros: Higher pay than competitive companies.
cons: Horrible training program. The first 1.5 months I literally had nothing to do. The department was too busy to train me in. Forget about lunch! You won't have time to take one. Management is hands down the worst I have ever seen in a company. In the 12 months I workes there my department had a 120% turnover rate in staff. That's not an exaggeration either.

title: Good bank, moving up league tables
body: Good bank, moving up league tables
pros: - Moving up league tables - Stable bank
cons: - Mediocre bankers - Bonuses not as stable as they used to be

title: Office conditions
pros: Not many good things to say.
cons: Small offices, extreme favoritism towards a part time employee. This person basically refuses to cover vacations. Weird how a part time employee can dictate to the branch director what she will or won't do and what hours she will or won't work. Last I checked, the Branch Director is the boss.
role: Current employee - Branch Service Manager, Senior Registered Client Associate

title: Meh...
pros: I was satisfied with the work-life balance. The work environment is pleasant. People are generally nice You're not pressured like other organizations.
cons: RBC Capital Markets is not considered a "top tier" bank in the United States, and it shows in SOME of the colleagues I've had the pleasure to work with.

title: RBCWM is a beneficial work environment
body: RBCWM is a beneficial work environment
pros: PCG pays for food and phone
cons: work can be dull at times

title: Decent Company and moving up
pros: Taking share and generally positive attitude (which is a nice break from a lot of places on Wall Street today)
cons: Pay is slightly below the comparable Wall Street firms (for associates). Promotions and ability to move up is no better than other places.

title: Great company, great people, fluid strategy
body: Great company, great people, fluid strategy
pros: RBC is a rock solid company. Pay is ok. Benefits very good. Long tenure of employees. Invests a lot in employees and the brand.
cons: The corporate strategy of the US firm, seemed to change every couple of years. Technology is some of the most antiquated on Wall Street, but they are catching up.

title: ok
pros: great place to work in the finance space
cons: not much room to grow - beaurocratic

title: Undesirable Salaries
pros: Great people, great culture. The workplace is like a family environment. The people here truly care about you and make this a comforting place to work at.
cons: The salaries provided are on the extreme low end. Not a lot of room for career advancement within the company.

title: Missing the boat
pros: Generally good people, decent benefits, organizational growth underway in US which opens the possibility of positive change. Financially strong parent provides a degree of security during bad times.
cons: Parent company is very bureaucratic, slow to react, lacks sense of urgency, misunderstands US market. Pinches pennies in all the wrong places - e.g. Technology platform is antiquated (quite literally, obsolete - IE 7, Microsoft Office 2003, major latency issues....make day to day activities daunting) and the organization is seemingly unwilling to fix it. Notable gender bias, although that is unfortunately not unusual for the industry. Mentally exhausting place to work.

title: A lot of work with no reward.
body: A lot of work with no reward.
pros: Great customer service experience. Very high paced and demanding of high organization skills. If you don't have organization, you will learn it quickly.
cons: Repetitious, fast paced customer service with many upset brokers that yell at you over the phone. High stress industry, Low base salaries compared to industry standards, and no growth potential.

title: Go to another financial firm
pros: Some of the people are nice to work with/for.
cons: The Management/Supervisors care more about liking you on a personal level than how hard of a worker you are. Also, every 2 years the employees take a survey about the management, but the last survey was so bad that it took management almost a year to tell us the reviews. Also, the branches take a review on the back office employees (which that is what I did) and even though the reviews were very good.....management said it could be better. It is like a compliment on one hand and a slap in the face on the other.

title: Great People, Bad Pay
pros: co-workers are great, very laid back atmosphere. Great PTO
cons: low salary with little room to grow

title: Colleagues were very nice however I believe staff was underutilized.
body: Colleagues were very nice however I believe staff was underutilized.
pros: Staff was great and never had issues with scheduling time off.
cons: Compensation was lacking and felt that the talents of a lot of the staff members were underutilized which limited any growth potential for employees.

title: Decent
pros: Decent benefits, lots of opportunity for the right people. No significant raises, 3 is the highest review score they will allow out of 5.
cons: Geared toward women and new college grads. A lot of the good jobs go to kids of the financial advisors. They do layoffs frequently.

title: Worked 8 and half years retired in January 2014. You did not have that year in your files
body: Worked 8 and half years retired in January 2014. You did not have that year in your files
pros: It is a very solid bank. Worked in private banking for a period fo 20 years with same executive. Would like to go back to work but not in private banking. My immediate superior can be contacted any time for references. Presently in Europe will return June 6, 2014.
cons: There is always training and I love the personal contact with the client. Have developed beautifu relatonships with them. You become part of a family.

title: Don't waste your time.
pros: For certain people there is work from home availability. Training is available if the budget allows. Yearly bonuses.
cons: On paper the company sounds good, but the work environment is poor. The morale is low. New employeses are shocked after they have been here for a month at the work flow & work overload. The pay is low & you could work 80 hrs/week and never catch up with work load.

title: 2nd tier US Investment Bank
pros: -Base compensation is in-line with the industry. -Equity Sales force is excellent at getting analysts in front of investors
cons: -Ibankers are terrible and lack basic knowledge of their industry, at least in the sector I covered -Due to the above the firm is left out of deals with companies where research has a good relationship -Given the weakness in banking, bonus pool is subpar -Technology investment is abysmal and the lack of investment reduces the productivity of the work force. For example, I still had Excel 2003 in 2013 and my job was to model in Excel. I had clients who couldn't open my models because our software was so old. I had another client poke fun at how ancient my laptop was. Its a broker/dealer, the aim is to impress the buy side. -Small US employee pool has lead to unsatisfactory employee health care benefits

title: Great company to work for, good benefits
body: Great company to work for, good benefits
pros: good salary, flexibility, great benefits, insurance and PTO, good corp culture, world wide presence easy to relocate. encourage employees to learn and challenge to learn new strategies
cons: vendor development which might not be up to the par when comes to enterprise compliance.

title: Terrific
pros: Wonderful place to work.
cons: No longer banking in the US market as of year end 2013.

title: They nickle and dime everyone.
pros: Some of the employees are not depressed about working there.
cons: Very provincial office. You would never think you are part of the world financial scene.

title: Working for RBC was always a pleasant opportunity to meet new people and expand my knowledge in my daily responsibilites
body: Working for RBC was always a pleasant opportunity to meet new people and expand my knowledge in my daily responsibilites
pros: Communicating with new faces every day!
cons: Pressure to assure money was accurate every day, and not making mistakes.

title: RBC Capital Markets
pros: I have been working there for more than 5 years. Standard benefits, somehow competitive with other banks.
cons: Off-shoring is happening and they will trying to move workforce to India. Few opportunities to keep your career growing within the organization. Directors and Head of divisions don't bother to get to know senior employees, you feel that you are one more working for a large corporation. Annual employee review is a joke.

title: Good Career Option
pros: Great benefits, great management, hard-working co-workers.
cons: Can be hard to get changes made locally with parent company in Canada.

title: My review of RBC
pros: While at RBC there was significant potential to contribute, so long you don't care about recognition.
cons: Company favors Canadians with much less accomplishments over non-canadians
role: Former employee - Director & Head Market & Trading Credit Risk

title: Great Place to work ... in Canada
body: Great Place to work ... in Canada
pros: The player in Canada so plenty of job opportunities.
cons: Growing player in US market so they tend to hire outside applicants and less apt to hire internally "projects".

title: The company overview of RBC capital market
body: The company overview of RBC capital market
pros: Competent and creative employees and nice place
cons: intense competition among employees, not cohesive

title: Too Much Micro-Managing
pros: Salary and benefits are competitive.
cons: Horrible management, concerns never addressed after escalations, micro-management to an extreme level.

title: Great place to work!
pros: The culture is friendly and supportive. Good support of balancing work/life.
cons: Driven primarily by RBC a Canadian bank leaving less autonomy.

title: A once great place to work slides into sub-mediocrity, and senior management bears the burden of blame.
body: A once great place to work slides into sub-mediocrity, and senior management bears the burden of blame.
pros: years of disparate patchwork technologies (all trying to run at the same time) lots of work (including after hours and weekend work) train wreck of a senior mgmt team that only looks at scuttling each other to advance. unskilled coworkers who are hanging on until someone notices.
cons: Zero IT vision or roadmap, becoming dependant on RBC to do everything (which they cannot). Skilled employees leaving on almost a weekly basis Zero accountability to customers/users unskilled ineffective managers at all levels Senior mgrs are terrified of RBC executives and throw you under the bus to save themselves. Chained to the RBC anchor which kills any technology advancement. ZERO morale (not that senior mgmt cares about that). Pay lower than companies 1/3 the size (benefits are ok though).

title: Weak culture of aggressiveness, lack of employee development
body: Weak culture of aggressiveness, lack of employee development
pros: Growing company that has a great vacation policy
cons: Aggressive culture that does not promote talent appropriately. Management is out of touch with many employee issues, it often loses talent. Most employees feel lost within organization.

title: Revolving door of personnel, always trying to upgrade but usually end up downgrading (NY OFFICE)
body: Revolving door of personnel, always trying to upgrade but usually end up downgrading (NY OFFICE)
pros: Decent work-life balance, not that competitive which allowed trading staff to punch out like the flintstones at 5:00 on the dot. Great domestic/canadian bank. Most of the revenue comes from the canadian banking operations, which is the core business and the most successful.
cons: No direction in capital markets, confused about what they were trying to accomplish (NY YORK). Mission statement is un-compatible with risk tolerance/risk appetite. Goals are to be in the #9 or 10 player in the market....who sets goals so low?? Very limited reputation in the US, limited visibility.

title: Good management
pros: Good management, pay for graduate school, smart people
cons: Low pay, not a lot of professional development, not everyone is motivated, lots of older employees, outdated technology, difficult to work from home

title: Senior management under cfo not up to mark compared to the top banks I have worked for in Wall Street
body: Senior management under cfo not up to mark compared to the top banks I have worked for in Wall Street
pros: Good pay Decent work hours Good work life balance
cons: Poorly managed place in the controlling side. Senior management is clueless in the controlling side so there is no motivation for anyone to excel. Worse they are transitioning jobs in controlling to Canada. So beware before joining here. Layoffs even though the bank is making money. Layoffs even for folks rated outstanding. Not because the bank is not making money. But because Toronto is cheaper. Problem I see folks in Toronto can't compete with folks in Wall Street. Management doesn't see that way though. Hope the us government makes them aware that there is a cost of doing business in us.

title: They no longer exist
pros: They had great managers. They had achievable requirement for conference trips. They gave you a book of business to work from which was extremely nice. That was my first introduction into insurance services and I didn't realize how good I had it from that standpoint.
cons: Their products available to sell were limited. Part of this was due to them being on the market to be sold. RBC had banks in the Southeast and had purchased Liberty Life Insurance Company as their insurance arm in 2000 and then in 2010 RBC sold Liberty Life and has completely pulled out of the United States.

title: Loss Mitigation Analyst
pros: Good company with great benefits.
cons: Management team was restrictive and limited. There was very little opportunity for movement. There were issues with certain managers, that HR seemed blind to. If you can get a good manager, this company is awesome. The pay could be more in line with the industry, they are a little low. And there seems to be a big variance on who gets a bonus.

title: Ok Place to work
pros: Lots of young employee's with little management experience
cons: Lack of management grooming for growing staff and business

title: Horrible and uncomfortable place to work.
body: Horrible and uncomfortable place to work.
pros: Company pays well and has a good reputation.
cons: They are obsessed with appearances, and really don't care about the quality of your work. For example, if you are at your desk at 7am and you don't get any work done, you are fine. If you come in at 9am and get all your work done, you will be berated for not being in earlier

title: Great place to work
pros: Very nice people and friendly atmosphere.
cons: There is no 401k match for FA's with production under 300k and there are several annoying out of pocket expenses the firm doesn't cover.

title: Badly Managed, Over Worked Employees
pros: Co workers are friendly and easy to get along with. Good Health insurance
cons: Bad Middle Management. No career growth. Expected to work overtime and get no recognition.

title: Noble intentions, more difficult in practice
body: Noble intentions, more difficult in practice
pros: Great people who are great at what they do Stability Useful resources Great reward system in principle Overall, a good place to work
cons: Very regulated and bureaucratic, which makes finding information a bit like going through a maze Little sense of connectedness

title: rbc
pros: great great great great great
cons: low wages no raises in sight

title: Program Manager
pros: Very Supportive environment, good benefits / compensation.
cons: Constrained by Parent company from Canada

title: Great brokerage firm that care about more then the bottom line
body: Great brokerage firm that care about more then the bottom line
pros: The people are amazing to work with.
cons: Sometime it feels that the US business is second to our Canadian counterparts.

title: Great place to work at
pros: Great environment,professional attitudes it is a great organization to work and grow within
cons: A little boring at times monotony

title: It was ok
pros: Learning banking regulations and rules. Customers were awesome, I loved working with them. Some good coworkers who became long term friends.
cons: Working at a bank on a small island lead to a LOT of people throwing other folks under the bus in order to get higher on the career chain.

title: Great company, lots of room for development and growth especially for someone willing to put in the effort.
body: Great company, lots of room for development and growth especially for someone willing to put in the effort.
pros: Exciting industry yet client focused. Great parent company cares about more then just the bottom line.
cons: Its such a large organization it can be difficult to see where you are individually making an impact.

title: Invest more in your employees
pros: It is a family environment. Love to have parties to engage people different personalities. You get annual raises and bonuses. The insurance was very good.
cons: Will work you to the bovne with no recognition or a pay raise from managers. The managers say they are supportive of your career goals, but definitely not the case. Morale is down and no insight from management. The software and technology for the company is behind.

title: Average Company Overall
pros: Great coworkers Nice environment Challenging work North Carolina Great history (previous company)
cons: Super bureaucratic Little bottom-up communications Management unable to tolerate risks Management worries more about process then work Poor management choices in recent years

title: I liked it.
pros: High payout. Access to capital. Good support. Good benefits. I liked working there was profitable every month I worked there.
cons: Management wanted to change my deal as soon as financial crisis hit. Was profitable every month, never took drawdowns, they didnt care. Raised commisions, lowered payout. So I left

title: Poor management in NY office
pros: Very large, successful organization. Canadian division is a great place to work and Canadian colleagues are talented and upbeat. NY offices are in Manhattan. Pay is ok, on par with other financial institutions, better than other industries.
cons: Note - this applies to NY office only! Poor senior management style and poor hiring of junior management. Very negative attitude and fear trickles down to staff. No promotions & no rewards for good performance. Over-hiring then firing demonstrates poor planning. Revolving door environment - good people leave.

title: The best company to work for so far. The employers are knowledgable.
body: The best company to work for so far. The employers are knowledgable.
pros: Compensation is better than most companies. The employees are knowledgable. Great learning opportunities.
cons: Havent experienced any so far.

title: Great
pros: Advancement opportunities offered across entire organization
cons: Incipient infrastructure which makes things harder to happen.

title: Talented, passionate people doing great work for clients - every day.
body: Talented, passionate people doing great work for clients - every day.
pros: Well respected company in the industry. Talented leaders across the organization who care about the advisors, staff, and home office employees, and most importantly about the clients. Strong, capable financial advisors that put client interests first. RBC is active in the communities which is serves - sponsorships, giving, employee volunteer opportunities.
cons: It can be challenging to get momentum behind projects/initiatives. RBC currently has too many layers of upper management including Regional Directors and the process to get everyone "bought in" is exhausting and convoluted. You feel valued doing your job, but there is no proactive energy put into growing employees or getting employees exposure to other ares in the organization to broaden their skills. Very difficult to move within RBC. Not uncommon to do the same job for multiple years.

title: long hours, conflict-ridden environment
pros: good financial position of the company
cons: very poor work / life balance poor treatment of employees sick days coming out of vacation

title: Very Good team to work with.
body: Very Good team to work with.
pros: Proximity to home, resonable pay.
cons: Very limited Growth potential, this location has very limited contact with business.Very limited Growth potential, this location has very limited contact with business.

title: Small bank thinking its a big bank
body: Small bank thinking its a big bank
pros: Great benefit plans,salaries are competitive
cons: Small company, incompetent people get promoted, lack of leadership direction

title: Nice and stable company, yet not too much opportunities to grow.
body: Nice and stable company, yet not too much opportunities to grow.
pros: This is for RBC Capital Markets: 1. Professional environment 2. Nice line of business 3. Job is somehow stable 4. Decent health plan
cons: This is for RBC Capital Markets: 1. Low bonus/salary raise 2. 401k is not the best 3. Disconnection between upper management and base employees. 4. Too many medium and high level managers 5. If you ask better salary/role they prefer letting you go. 6. Some IT corporate decisions make not sense. Getting new third party expensive software while wasting local resources. 7. Too many budget controls and activities per hour reports. Each activity has to be reported in 2 different control sites.

title: Enjoyable with minimal stress and opportunity to learn lots of new apps and tools
body: Enjoyable with minimal stress and opportunity to learn lots of new apps and tools
pros: great benefits, flexible, great teams to work in
cons: not much room for career growth,

title: RBC has been a great place to work - I'm going to miss it.
body: RBC has been a great place to work - I'm going to miss it.
pros: Flexibility to focus on what I'm great at. Allowed me to be creative in the ways I get the job done.
cons: I really can't think of any down side to working at RBC Bank except we're being bought by PNC. Change is good but I am not on board with the way we're changing.

title: so far so good
pros: profitable, consistent making money, good equity trading business, both prop and market making.
cons: need to diversity the business

title: Interesting, but need more challenges
pros: Good working environment and nice coworkers
cons: Not so flexible and lack of challenges

title: Great place to work
pros: In growth mode, lots of opportunity
cons: Bureaucratic, process-heavy, should be more results-oriented

title: Has a lot of potential, but senior managment in NYC is very lazy and not very knowledgeable
body: Has a lot of potential, but senior managment in NYC is very lazy and not very knowledgeable
pros: Name recognition Computer technology is pretty good
cons: Bonus sucks Takes forever to get promoted Cut-throat They hire smart people, so management can be lazy and collect big bonuses

title: Working for RBC Insurance was a demotivating experience.
body: Working for RBC Insurance was a demotivating experience.
pros: RBC Insurance had great products, but that is where it stops. The building had a small gym/exercise room and a cafeteria.
cons: RBC Insurance offered no opportunities for professional growth or career advancement. There was no information and knowledge sharing. It was like "us vs. them." Everything was kept at a hush-hush. It was a threatening environment. If we did not meet our outrageous goals then we were threatened with termination on a daily basis. The treatment of a team members was not equal. There were obvious favored teammates who did not have to meet their goals. By the way, I met my goals (killed myself doing it) and ended up quitting because of the low pay and even lower morale. This job turned me into a workalholic.

title: Well Rounded Experience
pros: Global Bank with a strong brand recognition Wealth management & Capital Markets growth Growth opportunities in career- you have to be the driver Group Executive in Canada are great leaders
cons: Exiting Commercial, Business & Retail Banking Lack of direction at RBC Bank USA Could not succesfully run and create a regional retail bank

title: Great group of people, really knew how to work as a time
body: Great group of people, really knew how to work as a time
pros: The energy and passion of the people I worked with
cons: Long hours, waking up early isn't great.

title: I certainly learned a lot in my time at RBC, but felt that management did not trust/empower employees to make decisions.
body: I certainly learned a lot in my time at RBC, but felt that management did not trust/empower employees to make decisions.
pros: Salary and bonus compensation was very competitive. The parent company has a strong reputation. They offer flexible work time arrangements.
cons: Senior management often did not seem to trust or empower the employees to make decisions that were necessary to properly perform their responsibilities.

title: RBC is an overall good place to work. They have good benefits and balance between work and family.
body: RBC is an overall good place to work. They have good benefits and balance between work and family.
pros: Benefits, balance between work and family, overall good treatment of employees, opportunities for advancement.
cons: Compensation is not the greatest, but that is usually true for all banks.

title: manage disressed real estate projects
pros: Working for a Canadian subsidiary allowed RBC Bank to have access to sophisticated loan products and services not offered by the competition. Leadership has become more effective in recent years allowing RBC Bank to be a competitor.
cons: RBC has had a tough time competing with other lenders. The brand was weak, the branch network is weak, and RBC Nank has had a tough time organically penetrating major markets.

title: RBC Capital Markets
pros: Solid Financial Institution. Good benefits. Nice people
cons: Significant turnover. Low base salary

title: Poor Management
pros: There are good benefits and not much is expected of you. That said, you can skate by and collect an OK paycheck.
cons: No focus. Senior management is an absolute joke. I had the craziest boss ever and I have no idea how this was not recognized before it was.

title: Branch management is good, District Management is lacking. DM wanted us to shove credit cards down client's throats.
body: Branch management is good, District Management is lacking. DM wanted us to shove credit cards down client's throats.
pros: great benefits, great bank accounts for employees, discounts with other companies like verizon or at&t, and of course... holidays are always closed days!
cons: all this job was sales, sales, sales. I am not against sales jobs, but don't do it under the cover of a title like relationship banker. At one time, banking was a respected position. Now not so much. Towards the end, our Regional President hired a District Sales manager from Wells Fargo and after that it became like a used car lot.

title: Traumatizing
pros: The medical benefits are OK.
cons: This may only be applicable to the specific department I was working in; nevertheless, the following occurred: -Poor communication between business units, senior management, middle management and staff -Favouritism -No room for advancement / growth opportunities -Salary was not competitive with the current market -Someone had previously complained about HR and senior management turning a blind eye to reports of incidents regarding mismanagement - this is TRUE. -Even if you possess the most herculean work ethic, you will not be recognized unless you are a brown noser. -Previous reviews mentioning self-promoters and "climbers" are VERY accurate.

title: Typical Regional Bank
pros: Work hours are offer good family/work balance...no work on Saturdays which is becoming more rare in banks nowadays. Paid holidays/Good benefits Advancement Opportunities
cons: Unrealistic weekly sales goals. Very high turn-over due to nature of business and goals. Training is one of the worst I have ever seen in any organization. Only work here if you can't find anything else and even then, keep looking...

title: Management Does not know how to run an effective bank.
body: Management Does not know how to run an effective bank.
pros: Job Stability if you enjoy no upward movement
cons: Management, No job growth, very reactive

title: Learned a lot about the financial services industry but dissatisfied with the pay and growth opportunities.
body: Learned a lot about the financial services industry but dissatisfied with the pay and growth opportunities.
pros: RBC is recognized as a top tier financial institution and there is a wide array of services they provide. The benefits are very good and you can learn a lot.
cons: All the departments are very one sided. They know their role and only know how to do it. It seems as if managers are afraid when a young candidates gets hired and actually picks up quickly on the job that their job is in danger. Therefore they refuse to promote them in any way.

title: Rewarding
pros: Challenging work environment and extremely talented individuals to work with.
cons: Long hours and high pressure environment.

title: A Good Company
pros: Co - workers are great to work with and all are team players. Pay is average to above average.
cons: Company doesn't advertise at all and you have limited products to offer your clients.

title: The experiences have prepared me to move forward within my career.
body: The experiences have prepared me to move forward within my career.
pros: Flexability in work day Great benefits
cons: Lack of support from managment

title: Great!
pros: Realistic goals, limited layers of management
cons: issues that any bank has that is not BOA with technology. LOB teamwork is a bit broken

title: Great place to work with great people.
body: Great place to work with great people.
pros: People and flexibility and time off.
cons: Not always sure what is happening at the different levels of the company.

title: RBC Bank is all about SALES GOALS
body: RBC Bank is all about SALES GOALS
pros: Most coworkers are good people. Bankers have many holidays off with pay. Banks usually have a good work environment, (air conditioned, desk to work at, semi-regular work hours).
cons: Since 2010, new government laws have caused a decrease in revenues for banks. They must make up for this lost revenue somewhere. Unfortunately, at RBC this has meant creating an environment of stress for the employees to "make a sale" and telemarketing their clients. Managers at all levels constantly throughout the day push for sales and warn you that you haven't closed enough deals for the day to reach your daily goal. Then there are weekly, monthly and quarterly goals as well for those in the retail bank branches. This is not unusual for bankers nowadays; However, consider this before taking on a job with RBC. It is a hard-core sales bank. If you like that, then it's the job for you. Before you accept a position, however, ask about the goals. Ask what percentage of employees in your prospective position met their goals the last quarter.

title: Going the wrong way!
pros: It is located in the middle of downtown Minneapolis and they help pay for transportation from the suburbs.
cons: RBC has some managers that are moving the company in the wrong direction. The back office systems at RBC have been down graded back in 2008. They thought it was an improvement, but they were wrong. They have taken away all the employe perks that were in place when I started and they don't even have Jeans day's ( which are free to the company)!

title: Rank Amateurs
pros: RBC has a sound balance sheet and is growing in a down market.
cons: Management (especially atthe functional and operational level) a joke. Latest strategy has resulted in a cadre of outsiders being brought in who are firing incumbents and replacing with their friends.

title: Grwoing Company with Oportunity
pros: The bank is doing extremely well as canadian banks have been insulated from the US real estate market The is alot of opportunity for movement within the bank
cons: Hours can be long as RBC attempts to compete with the top tier with some ground to make up in systems and initiatives that need to be implemented

title: Tired of budget constraints
pros: RBC Wealth Management strives to put a strong emphasis on customer service. This is important because in this day and age of Facebook and instant gratification, we tend to overlook "human" side of dealing with people. Pay was ok for my position level. The pay for managers and above were good.
cons: The operations side of the firm never really encouraged personal growth. Its almost like managers wanted you to focus solely on your job. Every company wants you to think they support you to further your career path. But I never experienced any manager asking about my career objective. Don't bother asking for support in taking any series exams. "Its not in the budget" will be the response you'll get.

title: RBC Insurance U.S.
pros: RBC is a strong company financially. Generally, the people in the Greenville office are terrific to work with. The home office is in a beautiful facility, and benefits are good.
cons: The parent company is not committed to growth of the insurance company in the United States. The U.S. home office has been troubled by weak leadership at the top and within the distribution channels..

title: RBC NY
pros: stable financials, significant growth and opportunity if culture can be navigated successfully.
cons: no cohesive culture in the US and regions operate in silos, which can be frustrating. ambition and drive not highly valued attributes.

title: excellent place to work
pros: on the investment banking side, firm has a great small feel to it (akin to a partnership) and a collegial atmosphere, where highly competent managers really listen to employees in order to help the business succeed. Managers' and company economically rewards employeesâ fairly well for their accomplishments.
cons: At times a bit parochial or provincial

title: Good for short term employment
pros: Fairly good benefits package Formed lasting relationships with my co-workers This was a nice "stepping stone" job right out of college (I would not have stayed longer than the 2 yrs I was there); good introduction to the banking/finance biz
cons: Performance reviews are a joke Advancement based more on who you know higher up rather than your work ethic Yearly salary increases were extremely low Tend to hire from outside rather than promote from within Management is quick to throw the "little people" under the bus rather than take responsibility

title: All depends on who your manager is, mine was absolutely horrible.
body: All depends on who your manager is, mine was absolutely horrible.
pros: None that I can think of.
cons: Middle management had no idea what they are doing and it is the most disorganized company that I have ever worked for.

title: Growing brand, needs to ramp up the business
body: Growing brand, needs to ramp up the business
pros: - Shop pulled in people from all over the street in all areas of Capital Markets across Equities and Fixed Income - Good place to start out your career as there is room for advancement;
cons: - Not all groups are built out so the firm lacks the reputation - Room for improvement in product base and accountability for management teams

title: Great place to work
pros: - Great culture for an investment bank - Small and dynamic groups - Access to senior bankers - Growing franchise - Relatively low turnover
cons: - Growing amount of politics within the office - No coherent tactics to follow the broader strategy to expand within the US

title: Good place but tough growing pains
body: Good place but tough growing pains
pros: High performers are able to move up through the corporate structure. Cash incentives for top performers was adequate to great.
cons: Senior management is asleep at the wheel. Recent consulting firm has chopped up and redone much of the leadership structure.

title: Canadian Goldman Sachs is irrelevant since there is no competitiion in Canada
body: Canadian Goldman Sachs is irrelevant since there is no competitiion in Canada
pros: Compensation was okay but performance metrics always changed. Generally a user friendly work environment.
cons: Canadians in senior US positions rarely understood US market. Promotions were arbitrary and had little to do with performance. Politics got in the way of common sense.

title: Poor Management
pros: Money was good/ competitive because they were so close to Raleigh but still in a small city. Decent bonus structure, too.
cons: About 80% of USA IT staff were contractors. Very high turnover of regular staff. People constantly moving around because nowhere was good for settling. Management was the reason, poor management all around. Highly political and they expected you to work the same hours as contractors who were getting paid hundreds by the hour.

title: Overall good place to work, however, has some current challenges ahead.
body: Overall good place to work, however, has some current challenges ahead.
pros: Good benefits. Good team members in the trenches.
cons: Strategy and culture is adrift - diluted by too little promotion from within and too much external influence without assimilation or creation of core culture and strategy.

title: The truth
pros: Management recognizes importance of work life balance. Compensation is better than BOA and Wachovia.
cons: Very little opportunity to move up. Everything is focused on making Scorecard #'s. Reorganizations happen every 6 months or so. When your boss' boss is out playing golf most afternoons, while you're stressed out to make your numbers, it's not very motivating!

title: Great sales culture.
pros: They have a very good sales culture and do a good job promoting this sales culture throughout the branches and business banking.
cons: They are not strong operationally. Their backroom function needs improvement. RBC did not seem to have a good reputation in the marketplace.
role: Current employee - Senior Account Manager, Business Banking

title: RBC Bank in the Eyes of its Own
body: RBC Bank in the Eyes of its Own
pros: There are several "best" reasons to work for RBC Bank. First, our benefits are Wonderful and very affordable compared to other companies. Second, there are so many departments within the bank which provides us the opportunity to learn and grow within the "same" company if we choose a new path. Third, the pay is competitive with the market for related positions. Last but not least, RBC is located around the world so there should be opportunities in the event someone needs to relocate! Great place to work!
cons: There is no specific downside related directly to RBC Bank. Even though RBC is a great place to work, it still has "drama" within all departments. Of course, this is with any company. It does seem that certain departments get more support for initiatives and ideas than other departments. This has been expressed as one side of the bank being the "Dark" side of the bank.

title: Great job
pros: Great management and excellent resources
cons: Poor compensation and bad parking

title: Very comfortable environment to work in, the people are wonderful and there is no ego at RBC.
body: Very comfortable environment to work in, the people are wonderful and there is no ego at RBC.
pros: great group of people to work with
cons: There is too much divide. The firm is in the process of growing and with that come some growing pains. In a year from now when it shakes out it will probably be an even better place to be.

title: RBC Dain...i'm neutral
pros: I had a great manager and wonderful team.
cons: Inefficent at comtrolling client expectations. Your experience really depends on your direct supervisior. I started out with a good one and ended up with a not so good one.

title: Weak management in the US
pros: Not a bad place in the current environment on Wall Street
cons: Weak management. Management does not effectively communicate performance measures and changes them to suit the need at bonus. Management does not effectively communicate financial details of the business but focuses on the good news of 1 or 2 good thing- not the entire picture. Feel like management is more interested in protecting their job than anything else. Very political. If you are not part of the club you can expect nothing good.

title: Voyageur Asset Management Minneapolis
pros: Voyageur Asset Management, a subsidiary, is where I worked. The senior management team there was an admirable group, overall this was a boutique of highly intelligent and experienced folks. I appreciated the location of the home office, the environment and the outings as well as the people I worked with.
cons: The bureaucracy of moving lateraly or otherwise within the mother company was apparent. I didn't like many things about the way HR worked with employees, especially the ambiguity and lacking in the tuition reimbursement program at the time.
role: Former employee - Fixed Income Portfolio Administrator

title: Solid company with huge potential
pros: RBC is secure company that is well positioned to be able to weather hard economic times. International growth opportunities too.
cons: Old technology, leadership that is slow to embrace change

title: Great opportunity but must be willing to be flexible and expect transitions
body: Great opportunity but must be willing to be flexible and expect transitions
pros: The best reasons to work at RBC Capital Markets are for the job training and experience it gives with a big name backing. RBC invests a lot of time and money training their employees. Too bad they don't spend that same amount of time in retaining their employees. In several instances, I have found that RBC pays above average to the marketplace.
cons: Expect lots of transition as managment consistenly moves employees around. Also several peers have left the company.

title: Strong company with few hiccups is worth working for.
body: Strong company with few hiccups is worth working for.
pros: RBC is a very sound financial institution with many different products to offer. This lends itself to also having many job growth opportunities. The people are generally friendly and very helpful. I find that, for the most part, clients leave happy and are starting to spread the word in our area about our service. There is a very strong emphasis on placing the goals of the client as a number one priority. As with all sales roles, there are minimum expectations for mystery shops. Beyond that, there is an open door we can walk through to make each client interaction unique. All-in-all, RBC Bank is poised to be a strong choice when seeking employment.
cons: some miscommunications about expectations, frustrations when researching items that were more easily found when worked with competitor bank

title: Good place to work
pros: Best place I've ever worked. Enjoyed most of the people I worked with. Compensation and benefits were good. Company affords people the opportunity to demonstrate their capabilities, particularly compared to many other investment banks.
cons: Brand recognition is not what it is for other firms or what it could/should be. It is unfortunate, because I've worked at more "name brand" banks where the people were highly incompetent and where the work was poor - at RBC the brand recognition isn't as good, but I felt the people and the work we did was significantly better than at other "name brand" banks.
role: Former employee - Investment Banking Vice President

title: Good work/life balance
pros: Stable platform due to parent company, so less concerns about being layed off. In research, broad industry coverage and senior analysts are reluctant as they know as long as they do a decent job, they will be compensated well. The associates have little to no room to grow unless in rare cases, analysts are fired, and even then it is unlikely that associate gets promoted. Not the same stress and hours of bulge brackets and is likely what makes people stay around and build longer term careers there (again, referring to more senior analysts).
cons: At the lowest-end of industry compensation. Research associates make $75-85k with ~$20k in bonus.

title: A Great Bank with Great Products but poor service.
body: A Great Bank with Great Products but poor service.
pros: RBC Bank is a solid bank with excellent products and good forward movement careerwise. They have a good work life balance philosophy. They have an excellent benefits plan including 401k medical vacation time off and short and long term disability. I enjoy that positions are very straight forward and not complicated. Cross training is done but not really encouraged. If you're a teller you're a teller if you're on the platform you're on the platform.
cons: RBC Bank has aggressively taken over more than it can chew it's acquisitions and hence the back office support is weak and poor service is provided to customer's internally and externally. Calling back office for support is difficult because no one is willing to take responsibility for issues that arise.

title: Weigh your options
pros: The non-salary benefits (such as health care, retirement matching to 6%, and tiered vesting schedule for matches) and the work life/personal life balance provide the most compelling option to remain here.
cons: Bank is increasingly hiring from outside the bank and the salary/promotion based on merit is not adhered to as much as it has been in the past. The bank is in a transition stage from a regional bank to make the move to a large bank.

title: Good firm to work with
pros: Very Bright People that are focused on the Financial Services Industry
cons: Brand name recoginition is low

title: RBC has it's good sides and down sides
body: RBC has it's good sides and down sides
pros: The company offers excellent benefits and it is a very friendly atmosphere as many of their branches in the south east were former community banks. There appears to be lots of opportunity with internal postings if you are willing to travel.
cons: Currently, the pay has been less than adequate and complains seem to fall on deaf ears. There is a lack of communication and it is very difficult to find who to contact in order to resolve any issues.
